metadata.summary: Write Django integration tests with pytest: DRF API endpoint coverage (auth, permissions, validation), transaction tests, service layer tests, and CI configuration.

content.title: Django pytest Integration Tests
content.summary: Integration tests for Django projects cover API endpoints, service layer logic, and database transaction behavior. Every DRF endpoint needs tests for authentication (401), authorization (403), happy path (200/201), validation errors (400), and not-found (404). Use APIClient.force_authenticate, pytest factories for data, and format='json' on all requests.

why.p: Integration tests verify that all the layers of a Django application work together: the URL router finds the view, the view calls the service, the service writes to the database, and the serializer formats the response correctly. Testing through the full stack catches integration bugs that unit tests miss — permission classes being misconfigured, serializers silently dropping fields, services not handling FK constraints. A comprehensive integration test suite is the primary safety net for refactoring.

list.item: Testing DRF viewsets, APIView, or Django views end-to-end.
Verifying authentication and permission enforcement on each endpoint.
Testing transaction rollback and atomic operations in services.
Verifying response shape (status code AND payload fields) after a successful call.
Testing filtering, pagination, and query parameters.
Pure business logic with no HTTP layer — use unit tests against the service class directly.
End-to-end browser tests — use Playwright or Cypress for UI flows.
Performance load tests — use locust or k6 against a real environment.

section.name: api-endpoint-testing
section.p: Every DRF endpoint must be tested for the full permission and validation matrix. API tests use APIClient fixtures and assert on both status code and response payload fields.

rule.statement: Test each DRF endpoint for: 401 (anonymous), 403 (wrong permissions), 200/201 (happy path), 400 (validation errors), 404 (not found). Use parametrize for validation test matrices. Always use format='json' and assert response.data shape, never browsable API HTML. Use authenticated_client and admin_client fixtures from conftest.py.
rule.rationale: API tests need to verify all security layers (authentication, authorization, validation). Parametrized test matrices cover edge cases without test duplication. format='json' enforces API contract, not HTML rendering. Asserting only on status code lets payload bugs through; always assert at least one field from the response body.

example.title: Comprehensive DRF endpoint tests

section.name: service-layer-tests
section.p: Service layer tests focus on business logic: correct output, side effects (emails, stock changes), and error handling. They use the db fixture and factories, not HTTP clients.

rule.statement: Test service methods directly, not through the view layer. Group tests in classes named Test{ServiceName}. Each test covers one scenario: happy path, error case, side effect, or edge case. Mock Celery tasks and external API calls at the boundary using unittest.mock.patch. Use pytest.raises(ExceptionType) with match= parameter to assert on error messages.
rule.rationale: Testing the service directly is faster (no HTTP overhead) and more precise (test exactly the service boundary). Class grouping keeps related tests together. Mocking Celery at the boundary tests that the task was enqueued with the right arguments without actually running it. match= prevents false passes where a different exception with the same type is raised.

example.title: Service layer tests

section.name: model-tests
section.p: Model tests verify string representation, auto-generation of fields, constraints, calculated properties, and state machine transitions.

example.title: Model unit tests
example.code:
# tests/unit/test_models.py import pytest from decimal import Decimal from django.core.exceptions import ValidationError from apps.orders.models import Order
@pytest.mark.django_db class TestOrderModel: """Tests for Order model."""
def test_str_representation(self, order_factory): """String representation includes order number.""" order = order_factory() assert str(order) == f"Order #{order.order_number}"
def test_auto_generates_order_number(self, order_factory): """Order number is auto-generated on save.""" order = order_factory() assert order.order_number is not None assert len(order.order_number) == 12
def test_total_is_sum_of_items(self, order_factory, order_item_factory): """Total property returns sum of item totals.""" order = order_factory() order_item_factory(order=order, unit_price=Decimal("10"), quantity=2) order_item_factory(order=order, unit_price=Decimal("25"), quantity=1)
assert order.total == Decimal("45.00")
def test_cannot_cancel_shipped_order(self, order_factory): """Shipped orders cannot be cancelled.""" order = order_factory(status="shipped")
with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="Cannot cancel"): order.cancel()
def test_cancel_restores_stock(self, order_with_items): """Cancelling order restores product stock.""" product = order_with_items.items.first().product original_stock = product.stock
order_with_items.cancel()
product.refresh_from_db() assert product.stock > original_stock

section.name: permission-tests
section.p: Permission tests verify that the access control matrix is correctly implemented: anonymous, regular users, admins, and resource owners each see only what they should.

rule.statement: Test all permission combinations for each endpoint: anonymous (401), regular user (200 or 403), admin (200), and cross-user access (403). Group permission tests in a class named Test{Resource}Permissions. Use separate API clients (api_client, authenticated_client, admin_client) from fixtures to represent each role.
rule.rationale: Permission tests are the security layer of the test suite. Missing a single combination (e.g., failing to test that regular users cannot access other users' resources) leaves a security hole. Class grouping makes it easy to audit which combinations are covered. Using fixtures for clients avoids setup duplication.

example.title: Permission test class

section.name: transaction-tests
section.p: Tests for atomic operations and rollback behavior require transactional_db instead of the default db fixture.

rule.statement: Use @pytest.mark.django_db(transaction=True) or the transactional_db fixture for tests that assert on transaction commit behavior, rollback on exception, or concurrent access patterns. Use refresh_from_db() after the operation to verify database state. Simulate concurrent access with explicit transaction.atomic blocks inside the test.
rule.rationale: The default db fixture wraps everything in a savepoint that is rolled back, so commit-dependent behavior (post_commit signals, ON COMMIT triggers) never fires. transactional_db uses real commits, enabling verification of rollback behavior. refresh_from_db() avoids asserting on stale in-memory state.

example.title: Transaction rollback tests
example.code:
# tests/integration/test_transactions.py import pytest from django.db import transaction from apps.orders.services import OrderService
@pytest.mark.django_db(transaction=True) class TestOrderTransactions: """Tests requiring explicit transaction handling."""
def test_rollback_on_payment_failure(self, user, product_factory): """Order is rolled back if payment fails.""" product = product_factory(stock=10) original_stock = product.stock
with pytest.raises(PaymentError): OrderService.create_order_with_payment( user=user, items=[{"product_id": product.id, "quantity": 2}], payment_token="failing_token" )
# Verify rollback product.refresh_from_db() assert product.stock == original_stock
def test_atomic_stock_update(self, user, product_factory): """Stock updates are atomic under concurrent access.""" product = product_factory(stock=1)
with pytest.raises(InsufficientStockError): with transaction.atomic(): OrderService.create_order( user=user, items=[{"product_id": product.id, "quantity": 1}] ) # Simulate another process taking the stock product.stock = 0 product.save() # This should fail OrderService.create_order( user=user, items=[{"product_id": product.id, "quantity": 1}] )

section.name: celery-task-tests
section.p: Testing Celery tasks in isolation: verify that the task performs its job correctly and handles missing data gracefully.

example.title: Celery task tests

section.name: ci-configuration
section.p: CI must run tests with the production database engine (Postgres) to catch vendor-specific bugs. SQLite is fine for local unit tests but not for CI.

rule.statement: Run CI tests against PostgreSQL (same engine as production), not SQLite. Use a GitHub Actions services block for the Postgres container. Run pytest -n auto --cov=apps --cov-report=xml --cov-fail-under=80. Upload coverage XML to Codecov or equivalent. Cache pip dependencies to reduce CI time.
rule.rationale: Tests passing on SQLite can fail on Postgres due to JSONField ordering differences, FK integrity timing, partial index behaviour, and case sensitivity. Running CI against the production engine prevents surprise failures on deployment. Coverage upload enables PR coverage diff comments.

example.title: GitHub Actions CI for Django tests
example.code:
# .github/workflows/test.yml name: Tests
on: push: branches: [main, develop] pull_request: branches: [main, develop]
env: PYTHON_VERSION: "3.11" DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: config.settings.test_postgres
jobs: test: runs-on: ubuntu-latest
services: postgres: image: postgres:15 env: POSTGRES_USER: postgres POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres POSTGRES_DB: test_db ports: - 5432:5432 options: >- --health-cmd pg_isready --health-interval 10s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 5
steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4
- name: Set up Python uses: actions/setup-python@v5 with: python-version: ${{ env.PYTHON_VERSION }} cache: "pip"
- name: Install dependencies run: | python -m pip install --upgrade pip pip install -r requirements/test.txt
- name: Run tests env: DATABASE_URL: postgres://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/test_db run: | pytest -n auto --cov=apps --cov-report=xml --cov-fail-under=80
- name: Upload coverage uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4 with: file: ./coverage.xml fail_ci_if_error: true

section.name: aaa-pattern
section.p: Every test follows Arrange-Act-Assert: set up test data, perform the action, verify the result. One logical assertion per test.

rule.statement: One logical concept per test. Multiple assertions on the same concept are allowed. Asserts on different concerns should split into separate tests. Use the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern: Arrange (setup), Act (call the unit under test), Assert (verify the result). Each test should have one clear reason to fail. Test behavior, not implementation: verify observable outcomes, not internal method calls.
rule.rationale: Tests with multiple concerns fail for multiple reasons, making debugging harder. AAA clarifies test intent. One assertion focus makes test names precise and prevents masking of multiple bugs. Testing observable behavior makes tests survive refactoring; testing internals makes tests break on every refactor.

example.title: Arrange-Act-Assert pattern
example.code:
@pytest.mark.django_db def test_order_total_calculation(order_factory, product): # Arrange - setup test data order = order_factory(items=[{"product": product, "quantity": 2}])
# Act - perform action total = order.calculate_total()
# Assert - verify result assert total == product.price * 2
# Good: Testing observable behavior def test_create_order_rejects_empty_cart(user): with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="Cart is empty"): service.create_order(user, items=[])
# Bad: Testing internal implementation def test_create_order_calls_validate(user): with patch.object(service, '_validate') as mock: service.create_order(user, items=[]) mock.assert_called() # Breaks on every refactor

antipattern.description: assert response.status_code == 200 without checking the response body.
antipattern.reason: A 200 response with an empty or malformed body is a bug. Always assert at least one field from response.data to verify the contract: assert response.data["email"] == user.email.

antipattern.description: Testing through the view layer to test service logic.
antipattern.reason: View tests are slow (HTTP overhead) and test many things at once. Test service methods directly for business logic. Integration tests verify the end-to-end contract; they don't replace unit tests on the service.

checklist.check: Every DRF endpoint tested for 401, 403, 200/201, 400, and 404
format='json' used on every API request
response.data fields asserted, not only status code
Service methods tested directly, not only through HTTP
Transaction tests use transactional_db or django_db(transaction=True)
refresh_from_db() called after state-changing operations to verify DB state
Celery tasks mocked at the boundary (apps.module.tasks.send_task), not definition site
CI runs tests against PostgreSQL, not SQLite
pytest --collect-only smoke check in CI to catch import errors before running tests
